notes.text: H1 KEYWORD ALIGNMENT: Your restructured body H1 reads 'How to Deploy AI Agents for Accounts Payable: 4-Step Implementation Roadmap' but the primary target keyword is 'AI accounts payable automation.' Google indexes the body H1, not the frontmatter title. Align the two so the H1 in the article body matches the frontmatter title: 'AI Accounts Payable Automation: 7-Step Implementation Guide' works perfectly and follows the SEO H1 formula. Never let the body H1 and frontmatter title diverge.

BOF FUNNEL COMPLIANCE — STRONG EXECUTION: This article correctly includes numbered implementation steps (7 steps), names four specific failure scenarios, and has a clearly labeled go/no-go decision checkpoint section with four explicit criteria. The two internal links pointing to deeper content are present. The FAQ section has five well-sourced Q+A pairs. This is a well-structured BOF piece — the em-dash issue is the only meaningful barrier to a clean submission.

TOF word count discipline: This article runs approximately 1,050-1,100 words against a TOF ceiling of 1,000. Trim the 'Three Consistent Failure Modes' section and the 'Three Actions' section by combining related sentences. Target 900 words — enough for substance, with clear headroom below the limit. Word count violations are automatic -20 deductions regardless of content quality.

TOF H1 must be a question: The formatter published 'AI Risk Management Finance: Stop Nation-State Breaches' as the title — a declarative statement. TOF funnel requires a question-format H1 ('Can Your Security Stack Stop Nation-State Breaches?' or 'Does Your Enterprise Firewall Survive a Nation-State Attack?'). The question-format title existed in your working draft but did not make it into the final formatter output. Confirm the formatter receives and preserves the correct H1 every run.

FAQ section missing from published article: has_faq is set to true in the SEO/GSO package and the FAQ pairs are fully written in the frontmatter, but the '## Frequently Asked Questions' section with its Q+A pairs was not included in the formatter article body. This costs -5 points and, more importantly, loses the FAQPage schema markup and featured snippet eligibility that the schema_type: FAQPage declaration promises. Ensure the FAQ block is appended to the article body before the Sources section on every run where has_faq=true.
(Director output was truncated — coaching notes unavailable this run)

TOF funnel H1 must be a question: The formatted article title is 'Chief AI Officer: Why Artificial Intelligence Banking Needs One' — a declarative statement, not a question. TOF articles require question-format H1s (e.g., 'Does Your Bank Need a Chief AI Officer? HSBC's Move Signals Yes' — which the Topic Selector specified). The Formatter overwrote this. Ensure the final YAML title matches the question format from the brief.

FAQ section is missing from the article body: has_faq=true is set in the SEO package and faq_pairs are defined in the frontmatter, but no '## Frequently Asked Questions' section appears in the formatted article body. The FAQ content exists in the [RESTRUCTURED ARTICLE] draft but was not carried into the final .md output. The Formatter must include the FAQ section as rendered body content, not just frontmatter metadata.

Add a second genuine question H2 to hit the GSO requirement: Only one H2 is phrased as a question ('Does AI Risk Management in Finance Require a Standalone C-Suite Role?'). The guidelines require at least two question H2s each followed by a 40-60 word answer-first paragraph. Consider converting 'How AI Is Changing Investment Banking Organizational Structures at Scale' into a proper question format, e.g., 'How Is AI Changing Investment Banking Organizational Structures in 2026?' — and ensure the immediately following paragraph opens with a direct declarative answer, not context-setting.

TOF lede must open with a concrete fact, not a generalization. The current opener ('Most finance executives assume...') is a setup paragraph, not a lede. Restructure the first two sentences to lead with the most striking data point: 'According to Gartner, 60% of AI deployment failures stem from insufficient pre-production validation — a gap that costs finance teams weeks of remediation and exposes them to regulatory risk.' Put the number first, then build the argument.

TOF funnel compliance requires a question-format H1 and a clearly labeled 'Clear Verdict' section. The current H1 is declarative and the verdict section is labeled 'Verdict: Validate First, Deploy Second.' Rename it '## Clear Verdict' to satisfy the funnel structure check, and consider whether the H1 can be reframed as a question (e.g., 'AI Risk Management Finance: Is Your Model Ready for Live Deployment?') without sacrificing the SEO keyword.

The MIT Sloan Management Review source ('An AI Reckoning for HR') is an HR-focused article being used to support a finance compliance claim. This is a sourcing mismatch that a skeptical reader or editor will flag. Either find a directly relevant MIT Sloan or equivalent source on AI validation in financial services, or remove the specific attribution and reframe the sentence as industry-observed pattern rather than citing research that does not directly apply.

The 'Midwest lender reducing manual review queues by 28%' claim cites 'AWS Bedrock customer documentation from Q4 2025' without a direct URL or institution name. In future articles, either name the institution, label it explicitly as an anonymized AWS case study, or link directly to the source document. Vague case study citations weaken credibility with a CFO audience that will try to verify claims.

The internal link anchor text in two places reads 'our analysis of the FCA's explainability requirements covers this compliance risk in depth at [how explainable AI creates capital risk under FCA rules]' — the phrasing is slightly awkward because the bracketed link text duplicates context already stated. Tighten to a cleaner in-line reference: 'The compliance exposure is substantial (see [How Explainable AI Creates Capital Risk Under FCA Rules])' so the anchor stands on its own for scanners who skip body copy.

The H2 'What This Means for Finance Operations, Customer Service, and HR Automation' handles three distinct use cases in a single section with no H3 sub-breaks. Add H3s for each sub-topic (e.g., '### Finance Operations: The Case for Fine-Tuned Open-Source at Scale') to improve scannability, give each use case its own GSO answer target, and help time-pressed executives jump directly to their relevant vertical.

FAQ rendering gap — fix before next run: When has_faq=true, the Formatter must output a '## Frequently Asked Questions' section in the article body with all Q+A pairs rendered as '### Q:' and '**A:**' pairs. Storing them only in YAML frontmatter means readers never see them, the FAQPage schema has no on-page content to match, and the GSO featured-snippet benefit is lost entirely.

Generic H2s undercut an otherwise strong BOF structure: 'Success Metrics' and 'Decision Checkpoint' are the two weakest headings in the piece. BOF readers skim for decision-support anchors — give them specifics in the heading itself. Rewrite to name a number or action: 'Three Metrics That Confirm AI Roadmap Recalibration Is Working' and 'Go/No-Go Checkpoint: Four Criteria for Reallocating AI Budget Now'. This also improves H2-level SERP appearance.

First H2 answer-first compliance: The 'Five Conditions' section opens with a framing sentence rather than a direct answer. For BOF articles targeting executives who skim, the first sentence after any H2 must deliver the answer, not announce that an answer is coming. Try opening with the most decisive condition first: 'Your AI investment map must be documented before any restructuring signal can be applied — without a function-level spend inventory, you cannot distinguish redundancy from capability.'

CRITICAL — Fix the H1 to include a primary SEO keyword AND make it a question: TOF articles require a question-format H1 per funnel compliance rules, AND the SEO guidelines require one of 8 approved primary keywords in every H1. A title like 'Machine Learning Financial Services: Can Your HR Team Use AI for Compensation Planning?' solves both problems at once. The Restructured Article draft your pipeline produced had the right instinct ('Can Your HR Team Actually Use AI...?') but missed the required keyword anchor.

CRITICAL — Cut the article to under 1,000 words: At approximately 1,150-1,200 words, this TOF piece exceeds the hard maximum by 15-20%. The FAQ section (5 Q&As) alone adds ~300 words that belong in a MOF or BOF piece, not a TOF awareness article. Consider moving the FAQ to a companion MOF article, or stripping it to 2 Q&As. The failure scenarios section is valuable but could be tightened to a single illustrative example at the TOF stage — save the second scenario for a deeper funnel piece.

ADVISORY — Strengthen the Gartner citation: The Gartner stat ('80% of enterprise companies deploying AI as core HR infrastructure by 2026') is used three times across the article but the Sources section only lists it as 'Gartner, AI as Core Fintech Infrastructure: 80% of Companies Go All-In. 2026' with no URL. Per editorial guidelines, every statistic must cite a named source with enough detail for the reader to verify it. Add the full report name and a URL, or reframe as 'Gartner projects' to signal it is a forecast rather than a published figure. This is currently your weakest attribution and the one most likely to draw an editorial challenge.

TOF H1 must be a question: The Formatter dropped the question-format H1 that was correctly drafted in the restructured version. For every TOF article, the YAML title field (which renders as the H1) must begin with 'Does...', 'Can...', 'Should...', or 'Is...'. A title like 'Can Your AI Agent Security Keep Up With RSAC 2026's Findings?' signals to the reader this is an evaluative piece, aligns with TOF intent, and matches the GSO answer-first structure — the first H2 then answers the question directly. Remind the Formatter to preserve the question format from the restructured draft.

Render the FAQ section in the article body: When has_faq=true, the FAQ Q&A pairs must appear as a '## Frequently Asked Questions' section with '### Q:' / '**A:**' formatting in the article body itself — not just in YAML. The five FAQ pairs are already well-written and tightly sourced; they just need to be surfaced in the rendered markdown so both readers and search crawlers can access them. This is a Formatter instruction gap, not a content gap.

The 'Clear Verdict' section label is a required TOF structural element: The closing section 'Act Now: The Gap Closes Before an Attacker or a Regulator Finds It' delivers a strong conclusion, but it must be labeled '## Clear Verdict' to satisfy TOF funnel compliance. The substance is there; only the heading label needs to change. Going forward, treat 'Clear Verdict' as a fixed section-name anchor for all TOF articles, the same way MOF articles require a fixed 'What the Research Does NOT Prove' section.

Source URL integrity: Two of your seven sources use URLs that look like landing pages rather than permalinks to specific reports (the Deloitte Insights URL and the Gartner document URL). For a publication that holds every stat to named-source standards, these need to be direct document links or replaced with the closest verifiable alternative. If the Deloitte '9.4 months' figure cannot be traced to a live document, reframe it as 'per Deloitte's 2024 analysis' and note that the full report is available on request. One unverifiable central claim can undermine reader trust in the entire piece.

MOF internal link funnel mapping: You have three internal links, which is excellent MOF hygiene. However, review which funnel stage each target article occupies. MOF rules require at least one link pointing up to a TOF piece (awareness-level content a reader new to the topic would need) and at least one pointing down to a BOF piece (decision-stage content for readers ready to evaluate vendors). Right now all three links appear to be peer-level or BOF content. Adding one TOF link — something like an explainer on what agentic AI is, aimed at executives unfamiliar with the category — would complete the funnel chain correctly.

Eliminate the Oracle/ServiceNow statistic repetition between Sections 1 and 2: The 34% procure-to-pay and 52% resolution-time figures appear in both the first and second H2 sections. In a 2,600-word MOF article, readers notice when the same data point recurs within 300 words. Consolidate: keep the full attributed figures in Section 2 ('What the Cycle-Time Results Show'), and replace the opening of Section 1 with a higher-level framing sentence that sets up the ROI argument without pre-empting the evidence. This tightens the article and makes Section 2 land with more weight.

Answer-first discipline on section openers: The paragraph immediately after your first H2 opens with methodology context rather than a direct finding. Per editorial guidelines and the answer-first check, the first paragraph under each H2 should lead with the conclusion, then support it. Rewrite the opener to something like: 'The SiliconANGLE research tested adoption velocity at Fortune 500 firms with public AI disclosures — and the sample design means its 12-to-18-month gap estimate is a floor, not a ceiling.' That one sentence delivers the finding before the explanation.

FAQ section placement: Your FAQ data lives entirely in the YAML frontmatter as 'faq_pairs', with no '## Frequently Asked Questions' section rendered in the article body. If your template auto-renders FAQ schema from frontmatter, confirm with the formatter that the output HTML contains visible Q+A pairs for readers, not just JSON-LD schema markup. If the template does not auto-render, add the FAQ section explicitly to the article body before the Sources section. The has_faq: true flag creates a reader expectation that must be met in the visible content.

Internal link funnel direction: You have four internal links, which exceeds the MOF minimum, but the slugs alone do not confirm which links route to TOF articles versus MOF versus BOF. MOF compliance requires at least one link in each direction (to_TOF, to_BOF, to_MOF). Before the next submission, annotate your internal links in your draft notes with their funnel type so the formatter can verify routing. For example, the Oracle vs Zalos comparison and the AP automation guide likely sit at BOF; the explainable AI and FCA piece may be MOF or TOF. Confirming this routing protects the article from a funnel compliance deduction on re-audit.
